course_description:
This course covers the foundational principles, processes and practices editors must master to acquire, develop and polish fiction manuscripts in the commercial publishing industry.

WRIT7070 Editing the Manuscript has been designed in consultation with editors and publishers working across the publishing industry in Australia. It introduces you to the practices and principles you will need for editing fiction in the commercial publishing industry, teaching foundational skills in structural editing, copyediting, proofreading and production design. The course is practice-rich: over the semester, you will work hands-on with real manuscripts and learn from industry professionals who have worked in publishing and the arts. You will also have an opportunity to view and analyse Fryer Library manuscripts by eminent Australian authors.

course_aims: This course aims to teach students the processes and practices, as well as the underlying principles, required for editing fiction in the commercial publishing industry. Students interested in careers in publishing will gain valuable editorial skills. Students interested in creative writing will gain valuable knowledge about the craft of effective prose.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand the commercial publishing process from acquisition of a manuscript to final proofreading.
Understand and apply conventions of structural editing.
Understand and apply conventions of copyediting and proofreading.
Develop skills in conveying assessments and advice to authors and industry professionals.
Develop basic skills in using contemporary desktop publishing software.

assessment_details.title: In class editing exam
assessment_details.task_description: In this task, students will complete a combined copyediting and proofreading exercise using a printed text (either a short story or non-fiction article). The exam will be completed during class time and is open book; students may refer to notes and reference materials, but only in printed form: no digital devices are permitted. Copyediting Component Students will copyedit the text to improve clarity, consistency, and style. This includes refining sentence structure, enhancing tone and readability, ensuring consistency in spelling, punctuation, and formatting, and making stylistic improvements appropriate to the intended audience. Edits should be annotated directly on the text. Students must also submit a brief rationale (2–3 sentences) explaining key editorial decisions and a completed style sheet (template provided) outlining stylistic choices made during the edit. Proofreading Component Students will identify and correct hard errors in the same text. These may include spelling, punctuation, grammar, formatting, and typographical issues. Corrections must be made using standard proofreading symbols, as discussed in tutorials. A reference sheet of proofreading marks will be provided during the exam. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 60 minutes Calculator options Any calculator permitted Open/closed book Open book examination - any written or printed material is permitted; material may be annotated Exam platform Paper based Invigilation Invigilated in person

assessment_details.title: Brisbane Writers Festival reflection
assessment_details.task_description: You are required to attend at least one session at the Brisbane Writers Festival and submit a short written reflection (maximum 500 words) on your experience. Your reflection should go beyond summarising the session and instead explore your personal and critical response to the ideas presented. Consider how the session relates to your studies in editing and publishing, what insights it offered into the literary or publishing industries, and how it may have influenced your thinking as a future editor or publishing professional. Your response should be thoughtful, clearly written, and demonstrate engagement with the event. You are encouraged to take notes during the session to support your reflection. A completed cover sheet with a Declaration and Acknowledgment of AI Use must be submitted with your reflection. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit via TurnItIn through Blackboard
TurnItIn Receipts:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
For written component, extension is available via the normal application process.
For the component which requires you to attend an event at BWF, please contact your tutor as soon as possible so alternative arrangements can be made.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Acquisitions proposal and meeting
assessment_details.task_description: For this assignment, you will read one novel from the list of debut Australian titles provided on Blackboard (see the folder in the Assessment section). You will take on the role of an editor who has just read the manuscript and is preparing to present it at an acquisitions meeting. Your task is to write a persuasive publishing proposal (800–1000 words) using the acquisitions proposal proforma provided on Blackboard, which is based on documentation used by a major Sydney-based publisher. In addition to the written component (worth 25%), you will participate in an in-class acquisitions meeting (worth 15%) during the lecture and tutorial in Week 12 and the tutorial in week 13, where you will pitch your book to the class, respond to questions about your proposal, and engage in discussion by asking thoughtful questions of your peers. Attendance at both acquisitions meeting tutorials is required, even if you are not presenting that week as being an active participant in the discussion and questions will be assessed as part of this assignment. The written proposal must be submitted to Turnitin after the second acquisitions meeting, and must include a completed cover sheet with a Declaration and Acknowledgment of AI Use, as per course requirements. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Structural report
assessment_details.task_description: For this assignment, you will choose one of the full-length manuscripts available on Blackboard to print out and work on throughout the semester. Your task is to write a structural editing report of 3000–3500 words, offering clear, constructive, and comprehensive feedback to the author. While you may decide on the overall structure and format of your report, it should reflect your editorial judgement and the specific needs of the manuscript you’ve selected. Your report should identify both strengths and weaknesses, provide specific suggestions where appropriate, and maintain a supportive and professional tone throughout. High-quality reports are typically thoughtful, consistent, and genuinely helpful to the author. You are expected to express yourself clearly, and your report should be carefully proofread and mechanically flawless. To guide your approach, you are encouraged to review and grade one or more of the sample structural reports available on Blackboard using the provided criteria sheet—consider where you can improve and what best practices you can adopt in your own work. A completed cover sheet with a Declaration and Acknowledgment of AI Use must be submitted with your assignment. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: Overview of the course Overview of the course, proofreading marks, and production schedules Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Week 1: Introduction to copyediting Resources and training for copyediting fiction Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04
Week 2: Acquisitions UQP Guest lecture: how are manuscripts acquired? Preparation for acquisitions proposal assessment item Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Week 2: Writing an acquisitions proposal Copyediting skills workshop, plus some activities geared towards your acquisitions proposal and meeting. Learning outcomes: L01, L03
EKKA holiday no classes this week
Week 4: Guest lecture Hear from a professional editor about their practice Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04
Week 4: Copyediting skills workshop In-class copy editing assessment task (10%) Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Week 5: Plot and Pace Story parts and their proportions + learning techniques to modulate pace Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Week 5: Save the cat! (Beat sheets and plotting) Plotting workshop Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Week 6: Primary and secondary characters Motivation and reaction, possibility and plausibility, drama and narrative interest Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Week 6: Proofreading skills workshop Time to practise those proofing skills! Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Week 7: Setting and context The role and development of context; identifying relevant elements including repetitions, integration, sufficiency and consistency Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Week 7: In class editing exam In class editing exam: please make sure you attend this class or have an extension request lodged. Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Week 8: Theme, tone and continuity Plot logic, continuity and other nuanced structural issues Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Week 8: Theming workshop Working on finding the themes in manuscripts Learning outcomes: L01, L03
Week 9: Authors, editors and publishing practices AT FRYER LIBRARY for tute and lecture: Explore edited manuscripts at the Fryer Library. Learning outcomes: L01, L05
MID-SEMESTER BREAK-No Classes
No class: GO TO BRISBANE WRITERS FESTIVAL (IN OWN TIME — NO CLASSES) Attend a session and the Brisbane Writers Festival to complete your BWF reflection assessment task. Learning outcomes: L01, L05
Week 11: Writing a structural report Learning outcomes: L01, L04
Week 11: Structural editing skills workshop Structural editing skills workshop Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05
Week 12: MOCK ACQUISITIONS MEETING (lecture and tutorial) You must attend this workshop and present your acquisitions proposal Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
Week 13: Guest author and editor Q&A Hear from current industry professionals Learning outcomes: L01, L04

Week 13: Mock acquisitions meeting II Tonight we'll be finishing off any final acquisitions presentations! Remember you need to come along for this one as asking questions is an important part of the process. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however, please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
